beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Gamma star at one point nine is the number that reframed the whole result for me, because it says a hidden confounder only needs to nearly double treatment odds to erase significance. I want to feel how robust versus fragile that is. Help me: (1) rerun the Rosenbaum sweep on a study with a stronger effect and compare where its gamma star lands, (2) explain in plain terms whether a gamma of one point nine is easy or hard for a real unmeasured variable to reach, (3) name a plausible hidden confounder that could hit that strength.

props.command: Gamma star at one point nine is the number that reframed the whole result for me, because it says a hidden confounder only needs to nearly double treatment odds to erase significance. I want to feel how robust versus fragile that is. Help me: (1) rerun the Rosenbaum sweep on a study with a stronger effect and compare where its gamma star lands, (2) explain in plain terms whether a gamma of one point nine is easy or hard for a real unmeasured variable to reach, (3) name a plausible hidden confounder that could hit that strength.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: You ran a matched observational study. The result is statistically significant. But you did not measure every confounder — you never do. The question is not whether your result is significant given what you measured. The question is how much unmeasured confounding it would take to overturn your conclusion. That is what sensitivity analysis answers. Rosenbaum bounds give you the gamma — the confounding strength — at which your p-value crosses five percent.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'how much hidden confounding would flip your result?'; Rosenbaum bounds; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Build a Python sensitivity analysis tool: implement Rosenbaum bounds, sweep gamma from one point zero to three point zero in zero point one steps, at each gamma compute the p-value bound, find gamma star where p crosses five percent, and call Claude to interpret gamma star in one sentence.

props.command: claude "Build a Python sensitivity analysis tool: implement Rosenbaum bounds; sweep gamma from 1.0 to 3.0 in 0.1 steps; at each gamma compute p-value bound; find gamma* where p > 0.05; call Claude to interpret gamma* in one sentence"

beats.narration_text: Read it. Gamma is the maximum ratio by which an unmeasured confounder could change the odds of treatment. At gamma one, no unmeasured confounding — your matched analysis is clean. As gamma rises, you are asking: if a hidden variable made one subject in a matched pair up to gamma times more likely to receive treatment, what is the worst-case p-value? When that p-value crosses five percent, you have found gamma star.

props.code:
# sensitivity_tool.py — Claude Code output

gammas = [round(1.0 + i*0.1, 1) for i in range(21)]
p_bounds = [rosenbaum_p_bound(gamma, matched_pairs)
            for gamma in gammas]

gamma_star = next(
    g for g, p in zip(gammas, p_bounds)
    if p > 0.05
)
print(f'gamma* = {gamma_star}')
# gamma* = 1.9
# Interpretation: a confounder of strength 1.9x
# would overturn the result

beats.narration_text: Run it. The line chart plots gamma on the x-axis from one to three, p-value on y from zero to twenty percent. The p-value curve rises from near zero at gamma one to near twenty percent at gamma three. The dashed red significance threshold crosses the curve at gamma one point nine. That is the answer: a hidden confounder of strength one point nine times would overturn the result. A credible study should have gamma star above two.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: rising p-value line chart with dashed significance threshold, gamma-star crossing marked in crimson

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to extend the tool: add a second curve for the one-sided bound and compute the e-value — the minimum confounding strength that would explain away the result entirely, not just push p above five percent. The e-value is a more interpretable sensitivity metric.

props.command:
claude "update sensitivity_tool.py:
  -> add one-sided Rosenbaum bound curve
  -> compute e-value for the point estimate
  -> print interpretation of e-value vs gamma*"

beats.narration_text: The updated tool adds the e-value computation. For a relative risk of two point one, the e-value is three point six — meaning an unmeasured confounder would need to be associated with both treatment and outcome by a factor of three point six to explain away the result. That is a much higher bar than gamma star of one point nine from the Rosenbaum bounds, which addresses only treatment assignment, not outcome.
beats.visual_intent: slate: terminal output showing gamma* and e-value with interpretation comparison

beats.narration_text: So sensitivity analysis does not tell you whether your study is confounded. It tells you how confounded it would need to be for your conclusion to be wrong. Gamma star and the e-value are two different answers to that question. A gamma star above two or an e-value above three is generally considered a robust finding.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: gamma* = threshold for sign reversal; e-value = threshold for full explanation; robustness benchmarks

beats.narration_text: Your move: take any observational study result you care about, identify the matched pairs or the test statistic, and run the Rosenbaum bound sweep. Report gamma star alongside your confidence interval. If your gamma star is one point two, your result is fragile. If it is two point five, you have something worth defending.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run Rosenbaum bounds on your study, 2) compute e-value, 3) report both alongside your CI

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. So sensitivity analysis does not tell you whether your study is confounded. It tells you how confounded it would need to be for your conclusion to be wrong. Gamma star and the e-value are two different answers to that question. A gamma star above two or an e-value above three is generally considered a robust finding. Your move: take any observational study result you care about, identify the matched pairs or the test statistic, and run the Rosenbaum bound sweep. Report gamma star alongside your confidence interval. If your gamma star is one point two, your result is fragile. If it is two point five, you have something worth defending.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Rosenbaum Sensitivity Analysis with Claude] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?

props.title: Rosenbaum Sensitivity Analysis with Claude
props.subline: if it is two point five, you have something worth defending
